text: The surgeon inserts the first port behind the scapula for a left VATS diaphragm plication, using a trocar with a camera for guided entry. The lung is visible in front of the camera tip, ensuring safe access to the chest cavity. A 10mm camera is used, and CO₂ is applied at 8mmHg pressure to depress the diaphragm.

Under direct vision, the surgeon places a second five-millimeter port for graspers and a posterior port above the diaphragm for the FlexDex needle holder. The FlexDex needle holder, a six-millimeter device, is inserted through an eight-millimeter port, demonstrating its articulation capabilities with wrist supports.

The surgeon uses a grasper to assess the diaphragm, which appears extremely floppy and thin due to the CO₂ pressure pushing it downwards. The diaphragm shows considerable redundancy, and the surgeon prepares to place the first placation stitches using the FlexDex needle holder. The needle holder allows for precise suturing with its wristed articulation, enabling horizontal mattress sutures to be placed anteriorly. The diaphragm is carefully doubled over itself to ensure safety, as it is draped over the spleen, with an NG tube in place to decompress the stomach.

The surgeon uses the FlexDex needle holder to perform a series of horizontal mattress sutures on the thin, overlapped diaphragm. The needle holder allows for comfortable positioning and precise manipulation, facilitating the throwing of knots with a two O braided non-absorbable suture. The suturing technique is demonstrated multiple times, highlighting the ease of use and stability of the sutures.

You can see all the advantages or what you need to do for a diaphragm is to be able to have great ability and flexibility to suture in any direction. And really, other than I'm using my needle holder in my left hand with the Flex-Tex and I'm using right hand With the robot, there is no real difference. So I really think in terms of the technical differences between this procedure you're seeing now and a robotic diaphragm placation, which I also perform and also have videos on YouTube if you want to see them. The main difference is time. It takes quite a lot of time to set up the robot. Access to the robot. We have limited access. We don't have our own robot. We need an extra port with the robot as well for the assistant to pass in the noodles. It's not very simple to constantly bring in and out the robotic arms. And bigger ports. The robot currently needs larger ports than what you're seeing here today. So to perform this with the FlexDex really you're going to need a five mil port for the cameras, use a five mil camera, second five mil port and then this eight mil port and perhaps as you know robotics would require one extra port. My SI has a thirteen millimetre camera port, twelve millimetre camera port and eight millimetre trocars and a utility port for the assistant would be twelve mils. And time is the other thing. It's definitely a longer procedure, robotically, just due to the positioning of the patient and the time taken to dock.

The surgeon performs additional horizontal mattress sutures on the diaphragm using the Flex-Tex needle holder, securing the sutures to the previous stitch to enhance the diaphragm's position. A single pledget is used to prevent suture cut-through. The surgeon efficiently switches instruments from a grasper to scissors, demonstrating the ease of instrument change in VATS compared to robotic procedures. The Flex-Tex needle holder allows precise needle angle adjustments, facilitating accurate suture placement.

And there's a lot of very helpful resources on the flexdex.com website. One American surgeon I was watching who has an expert practice in ventral hernias said one other thing that I don't have is three division. I think it is still very helpful to have three division if you can and there are some bats cameras that have three division and that is something that I did miss compared to robotics and the reason that is is because it's just a little bit more difficult to pick up the needle and and exactly knowing three dimensions where you are so if you do have the ability to do a bat style application with the flex next needle holder and you can get a three-d camera all the better And then the only other thing that VATS loses out on compared to robotics is having your ability as the surgeon to control the camera. So you might see at some stage on this video that sometimes it's not perfectly centralized, the action, and that is something that having an automated camera or a robotic system is an advantage of. Here's an image of robotic versus FlexDex knot tie. The reason for showing this really is just to show how similar it is and then finally cutting the suture. I think virtually identical really. With erotics, as you can see there, I use my assistants to cut the suture.

Really the perfect way to do a diaphragm placation I think must be with the FlexDex noodle holder with a three-D camera and if possible with a kind of powered camera holder such as Autolap or there's a new company called Endocontrol that has a voice-activated camera holder. That would be fantastic and then you've really built yourself your own surgeon-powered robot. What could be better? So back to the diaphragm placation. I like to do diaphragm placations as tight as I possibly can. There's kind of a rumor, or you hear it on videos quite a lot, that people say not too tight. I don't really understand what not too tight is. I suppose not too tight means the suture's tearing through, but if you're not actually seeing them tearing through, then I think the tighter the better, really. The tighter it is, the further down the diaphragm will be on the post-operative chest x-ray. I used to do it a little bit looser and the post-op chest x-rays were really quite disappointing in how little the diaphragm moved, although they did get some symptomatic benefit. I think the tighter I do them, the better the benefit for patients, although obviously I haven't done a controlled trial in that, but that is my impression, having now done about forty diaphragm placations. also abandoned pledges i used to very strictly do um ten pledges on both sides um i was very worried about cutting through but uh seeing the strength of these um i no longer think pledges are necessary and i got quite a few plural effusions and i just wondered whether having a large amount of teflon with a paralyzed diaphragm that's not moving might just be causing these pleural effusions. I still leave the drain in two or three days just because I'm worried about effusions, but I haven't had any effusions since I gave up using teflon pledges and just did long lines of suture. This is two-oh, and the reason it's two-oh is because I'm using a suture called a B-fifty, B-fifty-two suture, which has quite a small needle on it, and it's small enough to fit down the five millimetre port anteriorly. I haven't yet tracked down a zero suture with a needle this small, although if I did I would use it. There are some other methods of doing diaphragm placations. One's using the endo-stitch by Convidian and that does have a zero suture. And I think the stronger the better really for the suture. I think there is no advantage to a smaller suture at all.

The surgeon performs multiple horizontal mattress sutures on the diaphragm using a FlexDex needle holder. Occasionally, the suture snaps during knot tying, but no early failures are observed. The suturing process is visually faster compared to knot tying, which is more time-consuming.

are lots of different methods for doing a placation i wouldn't say that this is the only way although i think multiple sutures is a good idea i have done a redo diaphragm placation where i found a single protein suture that had fractured in the chest from another center and that one suture had failed and then the whole repair had failed so i think multiple sutures is good I also haven't really come to a conclusion on how long these repairs might last. The diaphragm is still paralysed and the large band of muscle above the suture line is still paralysed and the forces from the abdomen are still going to be present on it. So I do worry a little that the diaphragm will continue to stretch in these patients over coming years. So if the patients are quite young, by that I mean in their twenties or thirties, I tend to put a mesh over the top of the diaphragm just to try and make it last as long as possible. And sometimes I anchor that into ribs from inside by doing a large suture either around the rib or into the perichondrium.

One thing about the FlexTex needle holder is that like any new device or like starting robotics, you need to practice. No surgeon should expect to be able to get it out of the packet and just start using it. It isn't like a robotic arm. It doesn't exactly follow your arm movement because it's half a VATS instrument and half a wristed instrument. so the wristing follows your wrist identically but the movement in the chest is still as a vats instrument so when your hand goes up the instrument goes down and this requires some practice but luckily there's a lot of resources on the flexdex website and they even have a little low fidelity rig to help you practice and i'm sure everybody's got endoscopic simulation suites in their hospital or little day fidelity ways of practicing I certainly would recommend doing a little bit of practice before your first case once you've done one or even two you will have spent a good couple of hours operatively practicing, and this will mean that you wouldn't have to practice in the future, but it is something that you should invest a little bit of time just practicing.

So, here we are. Here's the final result. There's a lovely tight diaphragm. This person was delighted with the results of their operation. Their shortness of breath improved immediately. And there's the FlexTex needle holder just saying thank you very much, taking a bow from the audience. And I think it's done a great job. I'm sure you'll agree it's done great. So thank you very much for watching. And I'd love to hear some comments about that and what you think. Thank you very much.
